# Frame & Light — Episode
## Underwater Images That Feel Alive
Host: Leo
Guest: Ava

[00:00]
Leo: Welcome back to Frame & Light. Today we're talking about underwater photography, and I'm joined by Ava, underwater photographer. Ava, thanks for being here.
Ava: Thanks, Leo. Excited for this one — underwater imaging is something I've been thinking about a lot.

Leo: This episode is brought to you by Adobe. Adobe Creative Cloud puts Lightroom, Photoshop, and Premiere on every device you edit on. Get started at adobe.com/podcast.


[03:30]
Leo: Take me through the decision. How does strobe lighting change the calculus here?
Ava: The short answer is An underwater frame is built around backscatter management before it's about composition. We've seen this play out at teams using Peak Design. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Leo: Take me through the decision. How does strobe lighting change the calculus here?
Ava: Honestly, Strobe positioning at 45 degrees is the starting point, not the destination. Read the water column, then adjust. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Leo: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Ava: Yeah, so — Adobe Lightroom underwater is half about white balance and half about removing magenta from blue water. We've seen this play out at teams using Capture One. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Leo: That maps to what I've seen.
Ava: Outside of Profoto-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Leo: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Ava: Here's how I think about it. Peak Design's housings are good, but for serious dives I still trust purpose-built brands. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Leo: Let's start there. What's the most underrated piece of underwater photography?
Ava: There are two angles — Most underwater photographers I admire got there by spending hundreds of dives without a camera first. We've seen this play out at teams using Capture One. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Leo: What does the failure mode look like? What's the most underrated piece of underwater photography?
Ava: Here's how I think about it. The frame should reward the eye in the first three seconds, then keep rewarding it on the fourth look. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Leo: Say more about that.
Ava: And when teams adopt Capture One in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.

[12:00]
Leo: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: This portion is brought to you by Peak Design. Peak Design builds camera bags, straps, and tripods designed by photographers for photographers. Use promo POD20 to get started.
Leo: Welcome back to the show.

[14:00]
Leo: What does the failure mode look like? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Ava: Honestly, An underwater frame is built around backscatter management before it's about composition. We've seen this play out at teams using Capture One. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Leo: How do you know when you've gotten it right? How does Peak Design fit into this picture?
Ava: Yeah, so — Strobe positioning at 45 degrees is the starting point, not the destination. Read the water column, then adjust. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Leo: Where do most teams get it wrong? What's the operational angle most listeners don't have access to?
Ava: I'd put it this way: Adobe Lightroom underwater is half about white balance and half about removing magenta from blue water. We've seen this play out at teams using Capture One. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Leo: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Ava: Outside of Adobe-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Leo: What does that look like day-to-day? What's the most underrated piece of underwater photography?
Ava: There are two angles — Peak Design's housings are good, but for serious dives I still trust purpose-built brands. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Leo: Take me through the decision. Where do frame and underwater photography intersect?
Ava: Yeah, so — Most underwater photographers I admire got there by spending hundreds of dives without a camera first. We've seen this play out at teams using Capture One. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Leo: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Ava: Sure. The frame should reward the eye in the first three seconds, then keep rewarding it on the fourth look. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Leo: That tracks.
Ava: And when teams adopt Capture One in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.

[22:00]
Leo: Let's pivot — strobe lighting is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Ava: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around strobe lighting cross-cut everything we've covered so far.
Leo: That's a useful framing. How does strobe lighting change the calculus here?
Ava: Sure. An underwater frame is built around backscatter management before it's about composition. We've seen this play out at teams using Profoto. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Leo: What does the failure mode look like? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Ava: It comes down to this: Strobe positioning at 45 degrees is the starting point, not the destination. Read the water column, then adjust. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Leo: Where do most teams get it wrong? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Ava: Sure. Adobe Lightroom underwater is half about white balance and half about removing magenta from blue water. We've seen this play out at teams using Peak Design. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Leo: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Ava: Profoto is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Leo: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? How does strobe lighting change the calculus here?
Ava: Here's how I think about it. Peak Design's housings are good, but for serious dives I still trust purpose-built brands. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Leo: What does that look like day-to-day? What's the contrarian angle most listeners don't have access to?
Ava: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Most underwater photographers I admire got there by spending hundreds of dives without a camera first. We've seen this play out at teams using Adobe. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Leo: What does that look like day-to-day? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Ava: I'd put it this way: The frame should reward the eye in the first three seconds, then keep rewarding it on the fourth look. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Leo: Where does that break down?
Ava: And when teams adopt Capture One in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.

[28:00]
Leo: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Ava: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.

Leo: Before we wrap, thanks again to our friends at Squarespace. Squarespace makes building a beautiful, branded website actually pleasant — no plugins required.

Leo: Ava, thanks for the conversation.
Ava: Thanks, Leo.
Leo: That's it for today's episode of Frame & Light. See you next time.
